card.title: How to Start a Fire Without Matches
card.teaser: Your ancestors made fire for 400,000 years without a lighter. Here's the method that actually works.
card.coverImagePrompt: Close-up of weathered hands working a wooden bow drill in a dim forest clearing, a tiny curl of brown smoke rising from the notch, golden dusk light, cinematic

lessons.title: The problem isn't heat
lessons.body: Most people who try friction fire in the wild fail in the same place. They work up a sweat, generate visible smoke, and then — nothing. No flame. They conclude fire from sticks is a myth. It isn't. The problem is almost never heat. It's that the rest of the fire chain was never prepared.

visual.imagePrompt: Two exhausted hands resting on a friction fire set, thin smoke still rising from a blackened notch but no flame, late afternoon forest floor

lessons.title: The fire triangle, in order
lessons.body: Every fire needs three things in sequence: an ember, a tinder nest to nurture that ember into flame, and dry kindling stepping up in size from pencil-lead to finger-thick. Miss any one and the chain breaks. Beginners obsess over making the ember, then try to blow it into damp leaves. The ember dies in seconds.

keyTerms.definition: A small glowing coal produced by friction or spark, not yet flaming
A bird's-nest of fibrous dry material that catches an ember and blooms into flame

visual.imagePrompt: A neatly laid-out ground spread showing an ember, a tinder nest, and three graduated sizes of kindling, overhead shot, field-guide aesthetic

lessons.title: The bow drill, explained
lessons.body: The most reliable primitive method is the bow drill. A curved stick strung with a shoelace becomes a bow; it spins a vertical wooden spindle against a flat fireboard. Friction grinds dark, hot dust into a notch cut into the board. When the dust pile reaches about the size of a pea and smokes on its own, you have an ember. Stop drilling. Cup it gently into your tinder nest.

visual.imagePrompt: Labeled technical illustration of a bow drill set with arrows indicating bow motion and ember collection in the notch, warm pencil-sketch style

lessons.title: What the wood matters
lessons.body: Not all wood works. You want soft, dry, dead-but-not-rotten wood for both spindle and board — cedar, willow, cottonwood, aspen. A good field test: can you dent it with your thumbnail? Harder woods like oak will polish rather than grind. And moisture is the killer. Even wood that feels dry holds atmospheric water; survival instructors often split standing dead branches to reach the bone-dry core.
lessons.funFact: The world record for bow drill fire is under 6 seconds, set by bushcraft instructor Alan 'Bones' Halbert. Most beginners are thrilled if they manage it in 20 minutes.

visual.imagePrompt: A split piece of cedar showing pale dry heartwood inside a weathered exterior, laid on moss, natural light

lessons.title: Before you ever touch the drill
lessons.body: The single highest-leverage thing a beginner can do is gather the fuel first. Thirty minutes of collecting tinder, fine kindling, and bigger sticks — sorted into piles by size, close at hand — doubles your chance of success. Fire-making is 80% preparation and 20% friction. Bad fire-makers fight the wood. Good ones set the table so the ember can simply walk onto it.

quiz.question: Why do most bow-drill attempts fail even when the user generates smoke?
options: The tinder and kindling weren't ready
The ember produced too much oxygen
quiz.explanation: A fragile ember dies in seconds without a prepared tinder nest and graduated kindling to catch it.

quiz.question: Which wood is a poor choice for a bow-drill set?
quiz.explanation: Hard woods like oak polish rather than grind into hot dust. Soft, dry woods such as cedar or cottonwood are the standard.

quiz.question: What's the visual sign that an ember has formed in the notch?
options: A dust pile about the size of a pea that smokes on its own
A visible flame in the notch
quiz.explanation: Self-sustaining smoke from a small dust pile means the dust has coalesced into a glowing coal ready for the tinder nest.

sources.title: Bushcraft: Outdoor Skills and Wilderness Survival
Primitive Technology: A Book of Earth Skills
